title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the discovery of penicillin. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Accidental Miracle: The Discovery of Penicillin
content.text: The history of medicine is often portrayed as a series of deliberate, genius breakthroughs, but one of the most important discoveries of all time was the result of a messy desk. In 1928, Alexander Fleming, a Scottish bacteriologist, returned to his laboratory at St. Mary's Hospital after a two-week vacation. ||(41)|| [41]Before leaving, Fleming had been growing colonies of *Staphylococcus* bacteria on glass plates[/41]. Upon his return, he noticed that one of the plates had been contaminated with a blue-green mold. Most scientists would have simply thrown the spoiled sample away, but Fleming noticed something extraordinary. ||(42)|| [42]He identified the mold as a member of the *Penicillium* genus and called the substance it produced 'penicillin.' While he published his findings in 1929, the medical world was surprisingly slow to react[/42]. Fleming himself struggled to extract enough of the substance to perform large-scale clinical trials. ||(43)|| [43]It wasn't until a decade later that Howard Florey and Ernst Chain at Oxford University took up the challenge[/43]. They realized that penicillin had the potential to be a 'wonder drug' that could save millions of lives, especially with the world on the brink of another global conflict. ||(44)|| [44]To test their purified drug, they conducted a series of experiments on mice[/44]. Eight mice were injected with lethal doses of streptococci, but the four that received penicillin survived, while the other four died. This clear-cut result gave them the confidence to attempt the first human trial in 1941. ||(45)|| [45]Unfortunately, despite the success of the drug, the patient eventually died because the team ran out of the supply[/45]. Realizing they could not mass-produce penicillin in wartime Britain, Florey and Chain traveled to the United States to seek help from the American pharmaceutical industry. ||(46)|| [46]By the time the Allied forces landed on the beaches of Normandy in 1944, penicillin was being produced by the billions of units[/46]. It was the first time in history that more soldiers died of battle wounds than of infection, a shift that forever changed the face of modern warfare and medicine.

paragraphs.A: Indeed, [41] Fleming’s workspace [/41] was notoriously disorganized. It was this very lack of tidiness that allowed a stray mold spore to drift through an open window and land on an uncovered petri dish while he was away.
paragraphs.B: In the [42] area surrounding the mold [/42], the bacteria had been completely destroyed. It was as if the mold had created a 'dead zone' where the infection could not survive, proving that it was producing a powerful antibacterial agent.
paragraphs.C: This [43] lack of interest [/43] was partly due to the difficulty of the chemistry involved. Penicillin was highly unstable, and early attempts to concentrate it often resulted in the substance losing its potency before it could be used on a patient.
paragraphs.D: This [44] group of researchers [/44] turned their lab into a makeshift factory. Using everything from milk churns to bedpans, they developed a complex system of pipes and pumps to filter the mold juice and isolate the active ingredient.
paragraphs.E: The [45] patient in question [/45] was an Oxford policeman named Albert Alexander, who had developed a severe infection from a simple scratch from a rose thorn. After the first dose of penicillin, his recovery was nothing short of miraculous.
paragraphs.F: This [46] trans-Atlantic collaboration [/46] proved to be the turning point. US scientists discovered that by using 'corn steep liquor' as a growing medium, they could increase the production of penicillin by over a thousand times.
paragraphs.G: Many [47] other antibiotics [/47] have since been discovered, but penicillin remains the most famous. It paved the way for a new era of pharmacology where we could finally fight back against the microscopic enemies that had plagued humanity for centuries.

questions.explanation: A refers to Fleming's 'disorganized workspace', which explains the 'messy desk' mentioned in the introduction.
B describes what Fleming noticed on the plate (the 'dead zone' of bacteria), following the mention of the 'extraordinary' observation in the text.
C explains the 'lack of interest' and technical difficulties that followed Fleming's initial publication in 1929.
D refers to the 'group of researchers' (Florey and Chain) and their makeshift factory methods to purify the drug.
E identifies the 'patient in question' (Albert Alexander) for the first human trial mentioned before the gap.
F describes the 'trans-Atlantic collaboration' and the 'corn steep liquor' breakthrough that allowed for the mass production mentioned in the conclusion.
